Guide

Construction PM Software vs. General Project Management Software

Why Asana and Monday.com aren't built for construction — and what you lose when you try to make them work.

✓ Updated April 2026

General project management software — Monday.com, Asana, ClickUp, Trello, Notion — is excellent for software teams, marketing agencies, and professional services firms. It is not designed for construction, and if you're using it to manage construction projects, you're working around its limitations daily without realizing it.

This guide explains exactly what you're missing and makes the case for construction-specific software in plain terms.

What general PM software does well

Before criticizing it, credit where it's due. General PM tools are excellent at:

  • Task management with assignees and due dates
  • Team communication and status updates
  • Simple Kanban boards and list views
  • Document storage and sharing
  • Workflow automation for repetitive processes

For construction company administrative tasks (HR processes, marketing tasks, office operations), general PM tools are appropriate. The problem is when contractors try to use them for project-level construction management.

What construction PM software does that generic tools can't

1. Job costing and budget vs. actual

The most critical differentiator. Construction-specific software tracks your original estimate, committed costs (POs and approved change orders), and actual costs (bills paid) — all tied to specific jobs and cost categories. Monday.com and Asana have no concept of this. You cannot know in real time whether your concrete category is running over budget in a generic PM tool.

This isn't a feature gap — it's a fundamental design difference. Generic tools track task completion, not job profitability.

2. Change order management

Change orders have a specific workflow: the change is identified, it's priced and presented to the client, the client approves or negotiates, the approved change adjusts the contract, and the budget updates automatically. Construction PM software handles this workflow end-to-end. Generic PM tools have no concept of a change order — you'd track it as a task or a note, with no connection to your budget.

3. Subcontractor management

Construction involves 10–30 subcontractors per project, each with their own contract, scope, payment terms, and lien waiver requirements. Construction PM software manages sub bid solicitation, sub portals for document access, lien waivers, and payment history. Generic PM tools track tasks assigned to people — they have no contract or financial relationship framework for subs.

4. Construction-specific scheduling

Buildertrend's Gantt with task dependencies and automatic cascading delays is built specifically for construction sequencing (you can't frame before you pour the foundation). Generic PM tools offer Gantt charts, but without construction-specific logic for dependency types and how field delays propagate to downstream tasks. Procore integrates with Primavera P6 for critical path scheduling — there is no Asana integration for CPM scheduling.

5. Homeowner/client portal

Residential construction involves emotionally invested clients who want to know what's happening with their home. Construction PM platforms like Buildertrend and Houzz Pro have dedicated client portals where homeowners access their project without becoming full platform users. Generic tools have no equivalent — you'd invite clients into your workspace, exposing your internal task management, costs, and team communication to your clients.

6. Selections management

Custom home construction requires tracking hundreds of owner selections — flooring, cabinets, plumbing fixtures, paint colors — with allowance amounts and approval timestamps. This is purpose-built functionality in Buildertrend. It doesn't exist in generic PM tools.

7. Drawing and document management

Construction documents have version control requirements that generic tools don't handle. When a new architectural revision is issued, the old version needs to be archived but still accessible, and everyone on the project needs to know they're working from the current set. Procore's drawing management and Fieldwire's plan access are built around this requirement. Google Drive is not.

8. Lien waiver tracking

Construction-specific legal requirement: conditional and unconditional lien waivers from subs and suppliers protect the owner from double payment. Construction PM software tracks waiver status and triggers release of payment when waivers are received. Generic PM tools have no concept of a lien waiver.

9. QuickBooks integration for job costing

When bills are paid in QuickBooks, they need to allocate to the right job and cost category in your PM system for accurate budget vs. actual tracking. Construction PM software is built around this integration. Monday.com has a QuickBooks integration, but it doesn't understand the construction data model (jobs, cost codes, subcontracts).

10. AIA billing formats

Commercial construction billings follow AIA G702/G703 formats — schedule of values, stored materials, retainage calculations. Procore generates these formats natively. No generic PM tool generates AIA billing documents.

When generic PM tools make sense for contractors

There are legitimate use cases for generic PM tools in a construction company:

  • Pre-construction admin tasks: Permit applications, design reviews, HOA approvals
  • Marketing and sales pipeline: Tracking leads before they become active projects
  • Internal company projects: Equipment maintenance schedules, hiring processes, office moves
  • Team training tracking: OSHA certifications, software training completion

In these cases, the generic tool complements your construction PM software rather than replacing it.

The real cost of using the wrong tool

Contractors who use generic PM tools for construction management typically share the same experience: they build elaborate workarounds (custom fields, automations, integrations) that partially replicate what construction software does natively — and then spend ongoing time maintaining those workarounds. The "cheaper" generic tool becomes expensive in lost time and workaround maintenance.

More importantly, the critical features — job costing, change order tracking, lien waiver management — simply can't be replicated in generic tools. The budget vs. actual visibility that prevents you from losing money on jobs requires construction-specific software.

Buildertrend — Purpose-built for residential construction, covering the full project lifecycle from estimate to final payment. Not Monday.com with construction workarounds.

Visit Buildertrend →

Frequently asked questions

We already use Monday.com for everything. Is it really worth switching?

Depends on your pain. If you're losing money on jobs without knowing why until they close, that's a sign your job costing is insufficient — and generic PM tools will not solve it. If you're small enough that you can track everything in your head and business is consistently profitable, the transition cost may not be justified yet. But most contractors who make the switch to construction-specific software don't go back.

What about using Airtable or Notion as a construction database?

Some tech-savvy contractors build impressive construction tracking systems in Airtable or Notion. The result is still a custom-built system with no construction-specific features, no accounting integration, no lien waiver tracking, and ongoing maintenance burden. It's the right-tools-for-the-job argument — construction problems are better solved with construction tools.
